text: This video will illustrate a robotic right middle lobectomy in an 86 year old female with an adenocarcinoma of the right middle lobe.
Upon entry into the chest, we encounter multiple adhesions throughout which were easily taken down using the use of bipolar.
Once all these adhesions were taken down, the lung was folded up towards the apex to expose the inferior pulmonary ligament as can be seen here.
The ligament is taken down to the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.
And here some lymph nodes are encountered and sent for pathology and further analysis.
The lung is then folded anteriorly to expose the posterior hilum and some level seven lymph nodes here along the carina are taken and sent for pathology as well.
I think overall this was an intermediate in terms of difficulty for the operation.
The patient had some good amount of adhesions throughout the chest as well as post obstructive pneumonia of that middle lobe causing some scarring
to the pericardium as you'll see in a little bit.
These again here are some more Level 7 lymph nodes which are removed and sent off.
Once this is done, we take the apex of the right lung folded down towards the diaphragm, and this exposes the peritracheal space.
This is open, and multiple lymph nodes are encountered, and each is sent off individually to pathology for further analysis.
Here, a level 4R lymph node is taken out.
Slightly higher above, some level 2R lymph nodes are seen and removed.
Once this was done, we attempted to fold the lung over laterally and realized it was stuck towards the pericardium.
That long white structure that could be seen on the screen is a phrenic nerve, and I was unsure as to how much phrenic nerve was involved.
We went ahead and took down some adhesions within the fissure of the right upper lobe and right middle lobe.
And in doing so, you can see some reactive tissue there in the middle lobe extending towards the upper lobe.
Here you can see the right middle lobe stuck to the pericardium.
That long white structure on screen left is a phrenic nerve.
And it is pretty stuck here to the pericardium.
We use a combination of retraction as well as bipolar energy device to mobilize this middle lobe off of the pericardium.
And again, at each step we're assessing if their phrenic nerve at all is involved.
In my mind, I think the robot makes this a very easy dissection because of the precision and accuracy.
Also, very minimal bleeding.

Here again, we're approaching the phrenic nerve, as you can see there, and I'm just dissecting around to make sure that we're not injuring the phrenic nerve in any way, creating a small plane and bulging a little bit at a time.
All of the tissue of the middle lobe here felt pretty firm and adhered.
And routinely when we do middle lobes, our preference is to take the vein first, followed by the airway, and lastly the pulmonary artery.
But early on, I felt that this was not going to be possible to do it in this order just because of the way the anatomy and how firm the tissue was.
And finally, we're pretty free of that phrenic nerve there.
We use some blunt dissection to separate that middle lobe off of the pericardium.
So I did attempt to look in the anterior hilum at the vein and realized quickly that there was a lot of scarring and fibrosis around there, and I was unable to really mobilize it very well.
So I decided to go to the fissure here and dissect out the fissure a little bit and complete this oblique fissure anteriorly.
Here we're dissecting right on the ongoing pulmonary artery and creating a plane between the middle lobe branch as well as the basilar branch.
Also dissecting out here anteriorly to create that plane.
And I use a tip up here to kind of get right in front of that artery, turn the tip up and deliver to the other side.
And I'll take a cigar with me and essentially what that will do is that will protect my pulmonary artery from when I bring the stapler in to finish the fissure.
So here the stapler is being introduced, the pulmonary artery is being protected by that sponge stick and that fissure is then completed.
This better exposes that pulmonary vein for us.
I'm able to dissect it here.
and get around it.
